My Background

👤
Who I Am

I am a Controls & Automation Engineer with 17+ years of international experience across the United States, Middle East, Europe, and Asia. My career has been built around one core mission: walking into complex industrial environments — many with legacy or failing systems — and transforming them into modern, reliable, high-performance automation solutions.

I have worked across food processing, packaging, medical devices, water treatment, and robotics — always in high-stakes environments where production downtime has direct consequences. I bring deep hands-on expertise in PLC/DCS systems, SCADA, industrial networks, system commissioning, and project management, combined with the technical leadership skills to deliver results end to end.

I hold a BSc in Electronics Engineering from Azad University and an MBA in Engineering Management from Sharif University of Technology. I am a Green Card holder based in San Diego, CA.

Period 1 — Tetra Pak (2008–2015)

My career began at Tetra Pak — one of the world's largest food processing and packaging companies. I started as a junior service engineer and grew into a Global Service Engineer over seven years.

I was deployed to remote international sites across the Middle East, often alone, in countries with different languages and cultures. This was a time before reliable internet — I had to be completely self-sufficient. I was responsible for full production lines: pasteurizers, valve clusters, sterilizers, aseptic tanks, CIP systems, and packaging machines.

The stakes were high. Dairy products cannot be held — contaminated milk cannot be drained into the environment. Every hour of downtime had direct consequences. This environment taught me to think fast, solve problems independently, and always put the customer's production first.

Over time I went deep into every system — pumps, valves, piping, pressure, temperature, PLC/SCADA — until I could diagnose and resolve any issue independently. This earned the trust of the company and customers, and expanded my role into consulting, spare parts planning, preventive maintenance design, operator training, and documentation.

Junior Service Engineer
→
Service Engineer
→
Global Service Engineer
→
Technical Sales Specialist
Coca-Cola
Nestlé
Danone
PepsiCo
Unilever
Arla Foods
Fonterra
Kraft Heinz
Hershey's
Ferrero
Situation
  • Arrived at a remote customer site to commission a full production line — pasteurizer, valve cluster, sterilizer, aseptic tank, CIP, and packaging machines
  • The customer was not ready: steam supply had issues, electrical infrastructure was incomplete, and utilities were not in place
  • Dairy product could not be held or wasted
  • The standard commissioning sequence could not proceed as planned
Action
  • Rather than waiting or escalating, I restructured the entire project plan on the spot
  • I bypassed steps that depended on unavailable utilities and instead floated water through the full line — from reception all the way through to packaging — to test system functions, flow paths, valve logic, and mechanical integrity
  • This kept the project moving and gave the customer time to resolve their utility problems in parallel
Result
  • The project stayed on track
  • When the customer resolved their utility issues, the remaining steps were completed efficiently because the system had already been verified with water
  • No production time was lost unnecessarily and the customer's confidence was significantly strengthened
Situation
  • On an international site, an aseptic tank from a third-party supplier could not communicate with the Tetra Pak packaging machine
  • No internet
  • Limited communication with Sweden HQ
  • Tetra Pak support only covered their own equipment
  • The customer had no technical expertise
  • Physically touching the other supplier's machine carried significant risk — technically and contractually
Action
  • I tracked down a local representative from the other supplier and built enough trust to get their technical cooperation — despite having no formal authority
  • I gathered the integration requirements step by step
  • Rather than risking damage by intervening myself, I trained the customer's operators to follow the precise sequence I had obtained and coordinated the process step by step, acting as the bridge between both suppliers and the customer
Result
  • The integration was completed successfully without direct physical intervention on the third-party system
  • The production line became operational
  • The customer was empowered with the knowledge to handle similar issues in the future
Situation
  • Many customers had frontline staff who could not operate the machines confidently and could not read English
  • Standard Tetra Pak documentation was complex, text-heavy, and had no practical examples — making it unusable for operators and technicians on the ground
Action
  • I translated key documentation into the local language
  • I redesigned the training program for three distinct audiences — operators, technicians, and engineers — each with the right depth for their role
  • I enriched the documents with real-world examples and photographs attached directly to the relevant procedural steps, turning abstract technical instructions into something anyone could follow
Result
  • Customers' staff became self-sufficient in operating and maintaining the machines
  • Dependency on Tetra Pak field support dropped significantly
  • Production efficiency improved and the customer relationship deepened — they saw me as a trusted partner invested in their success

Period 2 — Tamin Online LLC (2015–2023)

After seven years at Tetra Pak, the country was under severe international sanctions, making it increasingly difficult for foreign companies to operate. Rather than wait, I made a decision: I would build my own company. At the same time I began my MBA in Engineering Management at Sharif University of Technology — the MBA gave me the framework and confidence to start the business. I ran both in parallel for two years.

I founded Tamin Online LLC as an automation system integrator. Tamin Online did not manufacture machines — we integrated, upgraded, and delivered complete automation solutions by combining the right equipment, control systems, and engineering expertise. Every engagement was a project with a defined scope, timeline, budget, and deliverable.

We handled three types of projects:

  • Service projects — troubleshooting, maintenance, and support to keep existing production lines running
  • Upgrade projects — modernizing existing machines — replacing old control systems, adding sensors, integrating PLCs and HMIs
  • Integration projects — the most complex — sourcing machinery from Europe, reverse engineering it, redesigning the control architecture, and delivering it as a fully integrated production line machine. The sterilizer example below is this type.
90+
Projects per year
95%
Success rate
$2M+
Projects delivered
Phase 1 — Customer Request & Scoping

Customer defines requirement — in this case a sterilizer with specific capacity and product specs. We define project scope, target automation level, and production line integration requirements. For customers who didn't know exactly what they needed, we arranged factory visits to similar facilities — once they could see a working line, their understanding became much clearer.

Product & Process
  • What product will be processed? (milk, juice, dairy, other)
  • Required processing capacity (liters/hour)?
  • Sterilization standard required? (UHT, HTST, aseptic)
  • Inlet temperature, holding temperature & time, outlet temperature?
  • Required flow rate?
  • CIP required? What sequence? (pre-rinse, caustic, acid, final rinse)
Integration & Line
  • Standalone machine or part of a production line?
  • What machine comes before and after? What signals are exchanged?
  • Communication protocol required between machines?
Automation & Control
  • Automation level required? (manual, semi-automatic, fully automatic)
  • Preferred PLC brand? (Siemens, Allen-Bradley, other)
  • Remote monitoring or data logging required?
  • HMI language?
Utilities & Site
  • Available utilities? (steam, compressed air, water, electrical supply)
  • Available electrical supply? (voltage, frequency, phases)
  • Space constraints for machine and control panel?
  • Safety or hygiene standards to comply with? (FDA, CE, local)
Phase 2 — Sourcing & Inspection in Europe

We briefed our consultant network in Germany and East European countries to locate a suitable machine. We traveled to the warehouse — in most cases the machine was completely offline. Based purely on engineering experience and visual inspection, we assessed mechanical and electrical condition, checked any available documentation, and made the purchase decision. Machine was then shipped to Iran.

Inspection context: Machine inspected offline in European warehouse — no utilities connected, no power available. Assessment based entirely on visual inspection, mechanical checks, and engineering experience. This list becomes the baseline for Phase 5 decisions.
No. System / Area Component / Part Qty Observed condition Status Initial recommendation
Phase 3 — Gap Analysis & Customer Communication

We prepared a formal gap analysis report showing current state, what was missing, how gaps would be filled, and typically three options at different price points — basic, standard, and full automation. This gave the customer control over the decision and made the conversation about value rather than just cost.

Phase 4 — Planning & Engineering Design

Process understanding first: product flow (raw milk → 135–140°C → held 4 seconds → cooled → aseptic packaging), CIP sequence, operating modes. Safety interlocks defined before any programming. Then full I/O list, control logic, electrical schematics, HMI screen design, and hardware selection.

TagDescriptionTypeRange
TT-101Inlet temperatureAI0–200°C
TT-102Holding temperatureAI0–200°C
TT-103Outlet temperatureAI0–200°C
PT-101Inlet pressureAI0–10 bar
FT-101Flow rateAI0–5000 L/hr
LT-101Buffer tank levelAI0–100%
XV-101Feed valveDOOpen/Close
XV-102Divert valveDOOpen/Close
XV-103CIP inlet valveDOOpen/Close
PMP-101Feed pump (VFD)AO0–100%
PMP-102Homogenizer (VFD)AO0–100%
HS-101Emergency stopDIN/C
ISA-18.2 Alarm Classification: P1 CRITICAL Safety / HACCP — automatic action. P2 IMPORTANT Process deviation — respond within 10 min. P3 ADVISORY Log and investigate.
Tag Description Pri Setpoint Likely cause Auto action Operator action Step
How to read this diagram: Product flows left to right (solid arrows). Utility streams (steam, chilled water) enter from above/below (dashed arrows). Sensor symbols (TT, FT, PT) show measurement points. The divert valve (XV-102) is the safety gate — it routes product to drain unless ALL sterilization conditions are met simultaneously.
Key design principles: All ★★ safety-critical signals use shielded twisted-pair cable with independent 24VDC loop supply. XV-102 divert valve is hardwired through the safety relay — fails to DIVERT on any power loss (fail-safe design). E-stop is hardwired independently of PLC logic. All analog signals use 4–20mA (not 0–10V) for noise immunity in industrial environments. PLC communicates with HMI and SCADA over EtherNet/IP. Legacy field devices use Profibus DP.
Phase 5 — Mechanical Restoration & Electrical Upgrade
  • Full cleaning, worn parts replaced, all moving parts greased
  • New sensors installed — temperature (PT100), pressure, flow, level
  • VFDs installed for pump and motor speed control
  • New control panel built — fully labeled, ferrule-numbered wiring
  • Machine transformed from manual to fully automated HMI-based operation
  • Spare parts sourced through Turkey, UAE, and China network under sanctions
How this list was generated: After Phase 4 engineering design was complete, the initial inspection list from Phase 2 was reviewed against the new process requirements. Each part was re-evaluated — some healthy parts still needed replacement because the new design required different specifications. Final decisions are captured here before procurement begins.
KEEP No action needed
REPAIR Refurbish / recondition
REPLACE Source new part
UPGRADE Replace with better spec
No. Component / Part Phase 2 status Decision Reason New spec / action Source
Phase 6 — FAT (Factory Acceptance Test)
  • Documentation package prepared and issued to customer before FAT begins: motion function book, HMI screen guide, I/O list, hardware walkthrough document
  • Hardware walkthrough — customer inspects control panel, sensors, pumps, valves, heat exchangers, and all mechanical components physically
  • Individual I/O verification — every input and output signal tested point by point and signed off on the test sheet
  • Manual mode testing — each actuator, valve, and pump tested individually via HMI before moving to sequence testing
  • Automatic mode testing — full production sequence run in automatic mode from HMI start to finish
  • Fault and interlock simulation — sensors disconnected, valves blocked, emergency stop tested — all safety interlocks verified to respond correctly
  • Water test — full process sequence run with water to simulate real production and verify flow, temperature response, and system integration
  • All deviations and open items recorded on a punch list — each item assigned to responsible party with target close date
  • All punch list items resolved and re-tested before FAT sign-off
  • Customer signs FAT acceptance certificate — machine approved for shipment to site
Rule: FAT does not start until every item below is confirmed and signed by the project engineer. This protects both parties — no time wasted discovering missing items during the test.
Machine readiness
# Check item Responsible Status
M-01Machine fully assembled — all panels closed and securedMech. Engineer□ PENDING

M-03All pumps primed and lubricated — rotation direction verifiedMech. Engineer□ PENDING

Phase 7 — SAT (Site Acceptance Test)
  • Machine installed at customer site — all mechanical connections, piping, and electrical connections made to site infrastructure
  • All FAT punch list items confirmed closed before SAT begins
  • Site utilities verified against design requirements — steam, chilled water, compressed air, electrical supply
  • Full I/O re-verification at site — all field instruments confirmed communicating correctly after transit and installation
  • Production line integration testing — all signals between this machine and upstream/downstream equipment verified
  • Full automatic sequence run at site — SIP, production, CIP — confirmed working in actual environment
  • Water test run at site with customer operators present — confirming all functions under real conditions
  • Any site-specific deviations recorded on punch list and resolved before sign-off
  • Customer operators walk through full operating sequence under engineer supervision
  • Customer signs SAT acceptance certificate — machine is officially handed over for production
SAT vs FAT: SAT is conducted at the customer site with real utilities, real production line connections, and real operators. Pre-SAT conditions are harder to control than FAT — the engineer must verify site readiness before the test team arrives to avoid wasted travel and downtime.
Machine installation at site
