Phase 8 — Handover & Training

Documentation package: electrical drawings, PLC program, HMI files, I/O list, motion function book, spare parts list, maintenance schedule. Training delivered separately to operators (HMI operation, alarms) and engineers/technicians (full technical training). Local language translation and photo-based documentation provided where needed.

Customer requirement

Our customers — food processing manufacturers — came to us with a clear need: better line availability. Their preventive maintenance was not working. Machines were breaking down between scheduled service visits, spare parts were arriving late or not at all, and unplanned stoppages were costing them real money in wasted product and lost output. They needed PM to actually prevent failures — not just happen on a calendar — and they needed parts to be available when required.

Root problem: The customers were operating under the assumption that "scheduled PM + OEM parts = reliability." Under sanctions, both assumptions were broken. We had to find out exactly where things were failing before we could fix anything.
Bottleneck What we found Impact
Wrong PM intervals PM was based on manufacturer calendar schedules — not on actual failure patterns in the customer's specific environment (product type, run hours, temperature, cleaning chemicals). Parts were being replaced too early or too late — never at the right time. Machines still failed unexpectedly between PM visits. PM cost was high but effectiveness was low.
No parts availability OEM spare parts were completely inaccessible under sanctions. Customers had no alternative source — when a critical part failed, the line stopped and stayed stopped until something arrived. Lead times ranged from weeks to months, or parts simply could not be sourced at all. Unplanned downtime of 3–10 days per critical failure. Production losses, product spoilage, customer losses.
No cross-referencing system Nobody had done the engineering work to identify which alternative parts from accessible manufacturers were technically equivalent to the OEM parts. Every failure started from zero — no knowledge base, no validated alternatives, no pre-approved substitutes. Each failure required full engineering analysis under time pressure, with a stopped production line waiting.
No logistics infrastructure Even when an alternative part was identified, there was no reliable way to get it to the customer's factory. No established import channels, no trusted carriers, no emergency routing options. Every procurement was a new problem to solve from scratch. Long lead times even when parts existed. No predictability. Customer could not plan production around maintenance.
Misaligned incentives The traditional model — we sell parts and service, customer pays per incident — meant our income went up when machines broke down. There was no commercial structure that rewarded us for preventing failures. The more reliable the machine, the less the customer needed us. No long-term partnership. Reactive, expensive, adversarial. Customer had no reason to trust our PM advice.
Solution — three parallel tracks

Once we understood the bottlenecks clearly, we designed a solution that addressed all five simultaneously — not one at a time. The three tracks below were executed in parallel and reinforced each other: better PM required better parts availability, better parts required a logistics infrastructure, and the financial model had to reward performance for all of it to be sustainable.

Solving: Wrong PM intervals + No cross-referencing system.
Instead of "replace per calendar," we moved to failure-pattern-based maintenance. Instead of "OEM only," we built a validated alternatives database.
Action How we did it Result
Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Collected failure history per machine, per customer, per product type. Analyzed which parts fail, in which sequence, and why — environment, run hours, cleaning chemicals, temperature cycles. Built failure maps for each machine type. Identified the real failure modes, not the assumed ones. Found that many "unexpected" failures had predictable precursor signs that a PM visit could catch.
RCA-driven Maintenance Kits Engineered specific kits per machine type containing exactly the parts that actually fail at the intervals they actually fail — not what the OEM manual said. Each kit included alternative part cross-references, installation notes, and torque specifications. Eliminated unnecessary replacements. Reduced PM cost per visit. Increased effectiveness — machines stayed running between visits.
Cross-referencing database Conducted technical audits of every machine in the fleet. Identified all critical consumable parts. For each OEM part: found technically equivalent alternatives from accessible manufacturers in China, Turkey, and European aftermarket. Validated fit, form, and function before approving. Built a database covering 90%+ of consumable parts per machine type. Reduced spare part cost 30–50% vs OEM pricing. Zero dependency on sanctioned supply channels.
Solving: No parts availability + No logistics infrastructure.
We built a parallel supply chain with three channels — each designed for a different urgency level.
Channel Method Use case Lead time
3PL Door-to-Factory Partnered with 3PL providers in Turkey and UAE to create a structured "Door-to-Factory" delivery network. Parts ordered, customs-cleared, and delivered to the customer factory without any customer involvement. Planned PM kits ordered 4–6 weeks in advance. Scheduled maintenance — predictable demand. 2–4 weeks
Emergency hand-carry relay For critical failures with a line stopped: activated an international relay network — trusted agent in China sourced the part and hand-carried it through pre-established transit nodes (China → UAE → Turkey → site). Each node was a trusted contact, pre-vetted and ready to act. Critical unplanned failures where line is stopped and no domestic source exists. Minimized Down-Time Cost. 48–96 hrs
Early-bird batching Offered customers discounts for ordering PM parts 8–12 weeks in advance. This converted reactive demand into predictable demand — allowing us to batch multiple customers into single shipments and dramatically reduce per-unit logistics cost. Demand management — reduces logistics overhead. Customer saves money by planning ahead. Planned
Solving: Misaligned incentives.
We stopped selling parts and service as separate transactions. We moved to a Production-Linked Revenue Model — our income was tied to the customer's line output. When their line ran well, we earned. When it didn't, we absorbed the cost.
Contract element How it worked Why it mattered
Unit-based costing Calculated "spare part value per 1,000 units produced" — converted maintenance cost from a fixed expense into a production variable cost. Customer could see exactly what our service cost them per unit of output. Aligned incentives — the more they produced, the lower the cost per unit. Shared interest in maximizing uptime.
SLA with line performance target Committed to a measurable Line Performance Target — typically 85% OEE. Written into the contract. Professional engineering service bundled into fixed fee — our team was on-call as part of the agreement, not a separate charge. Customer had a guarantee, not just a promise. We had to deliver measurable results.
Risk / reward mechanism Penalty: If performance fell below SLA, we absorbed all costs — extra service visits, emergency parts, engineer time — at our expense with no additional charge to customer. Reward: When the line performed above target, we shared in the efficiency gain through contract renewal and expanded scope. Eliminated the adversarial dynamic. Customer trusted us because we had real skin in the game. Every engineer on the team knew their service affected the company's bottom line directly.
Result
  • The model transformed Tamin Online from a reactive spare parts supplier into a strategic maintenance partner
  • Line availability across PBC accounts increased to above 90% on average
  • Maintenance cost per unit of output dropped 30–50% vs the previous OEM-based model
  • The performance contract created long-term relationships built on measurable outcomes — which was the only sustainable commercial model under our constraints, and also the most valuable one for the customer
Customer requirement

A food manufacturing customer came to us with a production line that had been running for years on manual and semi-manual operation — no PLC, no automation, no data. Every machine on the line was standalone, operated by a dedicated worker, with no communication between stations. The customer had five machines in sequence: filling, packing with weight check, sealing, combining, and palletizing with shrink wrap.

Their problems were direct: inconsistent product weight (no automatic rejection of out-of-spec packs), line synchronization failures (one station stopping caused upstream backup and downstream starvation), no production data (they had no idea what their real output, efficiency, or downtime was), and high labor dependency (every station required a dedicated operator just to keep it running).

Their requirement: modernize the line with automation, connect all machines together, and give management real-time visibility into production performance — without stopping production for a long time during the upgrade.

Starting point: Full site survey before any design work. Every machine inspected individually — mechanical condition, electrical supply, existing control wiring, available space for new panels, and what signals (if any) could be extracted from each machine.
Station What existed What was missing Main problem
ST-01 Filling On/off relay panel. Manual speed adjustment. No flow sensor. Flow control, fill volume feedback, line ready signal Inconsistent fill volume. No auto-stop when downstream blocked.
ST-02 Packing + weighing Mechanical scale with analog needle. Manual operator reject. No signal output. Digital weight signal to PLC, automatic rejection actuator, over/under-weight counters Out-of-spec packs passing through. No data on rejection rate.
ST-03 Sealing Temperature controller (standalone). Manual on/off. No seal quality detection. Temperature signal to SCADA, seal fault detection, auto-stop on temperature deviation Seal failures not detected. Bad seals reaching customer.
ST-04 Combining Manual grouping. Operator placed packs into combiner by hand. Conveyor speed control, pack count sensor, downstream ready signal Bottleneck station. Operator fatigue caused irregular grouping.
ST-05 Palletizing + shrink Semi-manual palletizer. Shrink wrap machine fully manual. Pallet counter, shrink cycle signal, full pallet alert to supervisor No output count data. Pallets left on line undetected causing congestion.
Solution — design approach

We designed a three-layer automation architecture: a local PLC at each station controlling that station's functions, a line master PLC coordinating all five stations via EtherNet/IP network, and a SCADA system at supervisor level providing real-time monitoring, alarms, and production reporting.

The upgrade was executed station by station during planned weekend shutdowns — the line continued producing during the week while we installed and tested each station's PLC offline. Full integration and live cutover was completed in a single planned 3-day shutdown.

Station PLC model Key sensors added Key outputs controlled Critical interlock
ST-01 Filling Siemens S7-300 CPU 314 FT-fill (flow transmitter) · Pack-present sensor (photoelectric) Fill pump VFD · Fill valve · Infeed conveyor Stop filling if downstream (ST-02) signals NOT READY
ST-02 Pack + Weigh ★ Siemens S7-300 CPU 315 WT-01 (digital load cell, 4–20mA) · Pack trigger (photoelectric) Pneumatic reject actuator · Conveyor · Counter increment Auto-reject if weight outside ±2% tolerance. Count good/rejected packs separately.
ST-03 Sealing Siemens S7-300 CPU 314 TT-seal (PT100, sealer bar temperature) · Position sensor (pack in seal zone) Seal head solenoid · Conveyor · Heater control P2 alarm to SCADA if sealer temp deviates ±5°C from setpoint. Stop if outside ±10°C.
ST-04 Combining Siemens S7-300 CPU 314 Pack count sensor (laser) · Full-group detection sensor Combiner conveyor VFD · Group pusher pneumatic · Downstream gate Do not release group to ST-05 unless ST-05 signals READY
ST-05 Palletize + Shrink Siemens S7-300 CPU 315 Pallet full sensor · Shrink tunnel temp sensor Palletizer cycle · Shrink wrap cycle · Full-pallet alarm beacon Alert supervisor and pause ST-04 when pallet is full and not removed
Three-layer architecture:
Layer 1 — Field (Station PLCs): 5 × Siemens S7-300 — each controls one machine, reads sensors, drives actuators, enforces local safety interlocks.
Layer 2 — Control (Master PLC): Siemens S7-300 CPU 317 — coordinates all 5 stations over EtherNet/IP. No physical I/O. Pure logic: line sequencing, interlock propagation, OEE calculation, data aggregation.
Layer 3 — Supervision (SCADA + HMI Server): Siemens WinCC — real-time visualization, alarm management, historian, production reporting. Communicates with Master PLC via OPC-UA.
Master PLC — Siemens S7-300 CPU 317
Function Description PLC tag / logic
Line start sequence On operator pressing LINE START on HMI: Master PLC sends READY_CMD to ST-05 first. Waits for ST-05_RUNNING feedback. Then sends READY_CMD to ST-04, and so on upstream. If any station does not confirm within 10 sec → line start aborted, alarm raised. LINE_START_CMD
ST01..05_READY_FB
LINE_SEQ_TIMER
Fault propagation When any station raises FAULT signal: Master immediately sends STOP_CMD to all upstream stations (prevent product buildup). Downstream stations continue running until their buffers clear, then stop. Fault source and timestamp logged to historian. ST0X_FAULT_BIT
FAULT_SOURCE_INT
FAULT_TIME_STAMP
Weight reject monitoring Master receives GOOD_COUNT and REJECT_COUNT from ST-02 every 100ms scan. Calculates rolling reject rate over last 10 minutes. If reject rate exceeds 2%: P2 alarm to SCADA + alert on HMI floor screen. If exceeds 5%: line pause + P1 alarm. WT_GOOD_CNT
WT_REJECT_CNT
WT_REJECT_RATE_DINT
WT_REJECT_ALM_P2
OEE calculation Availability = (Planned time − Downtime) ÷ Planned time
Performance = (Actual output × Ideal cycle time) ÷ Planned time
Quality = Good packs ÷ Total packs produced
OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality
Calculated every 60 seconds. Written to SCADA historian tag OEE_CURRENT_REAL. 		OEE_AVAIL_REAL
OEE_PERF_REAL
OEE_QUAL_REAL
OEE_CURRENT_REAL
DOWNTIME_ACCUM_DINT
Production counters Shift counter, daily counter, batch counter — all incremented by good-pack pulse from ST-02. Counters reset by supervisor via HMI at shift start. Pallet count from ST-05 incremented per completed pallet cycle. SHIFT_COUNT_DINT
DAILY_COUNT_DINT
PALLET_COUNT_DINT
BATCH_COUNT_DINT
Downtime tracking When line stops, master PLC logs: stop reason code (fault source), stop time, resume time, duration. Operator must enter stop reason on HMI if not automatic fault. Categories: Mechanical, Electrical, Material, Quality, Planned. Data fed to SCADA for Pareto analysis. DT_REASON_INT
DT_START_TIME
DT_DURATION_DINT
DT_CATEGORY_INT
OPC-UA Communication — Master PLC to SCADA
Protocol: OPC-UA (IEC 62541) — secure, standardized machine-to-machine communication. Master PLC runs OPC-UA Server (Siemens S7-300 built-in). SCADA runs OPC-UA Client. Subscription-based updates: changed tags pushed to SCADA at configured scan rates — no polling overhead.
Tag group Example tags Scan rate Retention
Critical process WT_REJECT_RATE, TT_SEAL_TEMP, LINE_STATUS, FAULT_SOURCE 500 ms 2 years
Production counters SHIFT_COUNT, DAILY_COUNT, PALLET_COUNT, OEE_CURRENT 1 sec 5 years
Station status ST01_RUNNING, ST02_RUNNING … ST05_RUNNING, each FAULT_BIT 1 sec 1 year
OEE components OEE_AVAIL, OEE_PERF, OEE_QUAL, DOWNTIME_ACCUM, DT_CATEGORY 1 min 5 years
Downtime events DT_REASON, DT_START, DT_DURATION — event-based, on change On change 5 years
HMI Server — Siemens WinCC / Siemens WinCC
Architecture: Central HMI Server hosts all screens, tags, historian, and alarm engine. Clients connect via browser (WinCC) or thin client stations. Two client points: supervisor office PC + factory floor 15-inch industrial touchscreen panel. All screens synchronized — same data, different access levels based on login role.
Screen What it shows Access level Operator can do
Screen 1 — Line overview Animated P&ID of all 5 stations. Color-coded status (green=running, orange=warning, red=fault, grey=stopped). Live pack count, current OEE, active alarm count, shift progress bar. Faulted station flashes. Operator + Supervisor View only. Navigate to station detail by clicking any station.
Screen 2 — Station detail (×5) One screen per station. Live sensor values, actuator states, local PLC status, last fault message, station counters. ST-02 screen shows weight histogram (last 100 packs, ±tolerance bands). ST-03 shows sealer temperature trend last 2 hours. Operator + Supervisor Operator can reset station fault (if cleared). Supervisor can change setpoints (e.g. weight tolerance, sealer temp).
Screen 3 — Production dashboard Shift totals: good packs, rejected packs, reject rate%, pallets completed, current OEE (gauge 0–100%). Line efficiency trend for current shift. Target vs actual output bar chart updated every 10 minutes. Supervisor + Management Supervisor can reset shift counters at shift start. Enter production target for shift.
Screen 4 — Alarm management Active alarm list (tag, description, priority, time, acknowledged?). Alarm history last 30 days. Alarm frequency Pareto chart — top 10 most frequent alarms by count and total downtime caused. ISA-18.2 priority display: P1 red, P2 orange, P3 blue. Operator (ack) + Supervisor (configure) Operator acknowledges alarms. Supervisor can shelve nuisance alarms temporarily with reason code.
Screen 5 — Downtime analysis Downtime log: every stop event with reason, category, duration, and who acknowledged. Pareto by category (Mechanical / Electrical / Material / Quality / Planned). MTBF and MTTR calculated per station. Trend chart: downtime hours per day for last 30 days. Supervisor + Management Enter downtime reason manually if not auto-detected. Export to CSV for management reporting.
Screen 6 — Historian trends Configurable trend viewer — operator selects any historian tag, any time range, any resolution. Overlapping trends supported (e.g. sealer temp vs reject rate vs production rate on same chart). Export to PNG or CSV. Supervisor + Engineer Add/remove tags from trend. Change time range. Export data.
Screen 7 — Shift report Auto-generated at shift end: total good packs, total rejects, reject rate, OEE, downtime summary by category, alarms raised/acknowledged, pallet count. Printable PDF or email to management distribution list automatically. Supervisor (trigger) + Auto-generate Supervisor can manually trigger report at any time. Review and approve before auto-send.
Alarm management — ISA-18.2 compliant
Priority Definition Examples in this line Response required
P1 CRITICAL Safety or product integrity risk. Automatic line action taken. Station fault causing line stop · Weight reject rate >5% · E-stop pressed · Sealer temp out of range >±10°C Operator must acknowledge immediately. Supervisor notified. Cannot restart until root cause entered.
P2 IMPORTANT Process deviation. Operator response required within 10 min. Weight reject rate >2% · Sealer temp deviation ±5°C · Pallet full and not removed · Combiner buffer near full Operator acknowledges and investigates. Line continues but condition must be resolved.
P3 ADVISORY Information. Log and investigate at next opportunity. Shift production target behind pace · Conveyor speed deviation · Daily counter milestone reached Acknowledge and note. No immediate action required.
User roles & access levels
Role Login method Permissions
Operator PIN code on floor HMI View all screens. Acknowledge alarms. Reset cleared faults. Start/stop line. Enter downtime reason. Cannot change setpoints.
Supervisor Username + password (office PC + floor HMI) All operator permissions. Change setpoints (weight tolerance, sealer temp, speed). Reset shift counters. Trigger shift report. Shelve nuisance alarms. Export historian data.
Engineer Username + password + 2FA (remote access via VPN) All supervisor permissions. Configure alarms. Modify historian tag list. Access raw PLC tags. Remote monitoring. Cannot modify PLC program (separate PLC software login required).
Management Read-only web browser access (WinCC WebNavigator) View production dashboard and shift reports only. No control access. Can receive automated email reports.
Phase Duration Work done
Site survey + design 2 weeks Full electrical and mechanical survey. I/O list finalized. Network topology designed. Cabinet layouts drawn. PLC program structure designed offline.
Panel build + program 3 weeks 5 station PLC panels + master PLC panel built in workshop. All programs written, simulated, and tested offline with I/O simulator. SCADA screens designed and linked to tags.
Station installs (weekends) 5 weekends One station per weekend. Install panel, wire sensors and actuators to PLC, test individual I/O. Station runs in parallel mode (old system still active) during week to validate new wiring without affecting production.
Network + master PLC 1 weekend EtherNet/IP switch installed. All station PLCs connected to network. Master PLC installed and network communication verified. SCADA server connected. All tags verified live.
Full cutover + commissioning 3-day shutdown Old manual controls removed. Full line sequence tested. Weight rejection calibrated. SCADA alarms tested. Operator training delivered. Customer signed SAT acceptance certificate. Line started production on Day 4.
📋 Placeholder — forms and checklists to be added here:
The following project documents will be inserted in this section once provided:
· Site survey form
· I/O list template per station
· FAT / SAT checklist for PLC retrofit
· Weight calibration record
· SCADA tag list
· Any additional forms shared by Ali
Result
  • After cutover the factory was unrecognizable from an operational standpoint
  • Line OEE increased from ~62% to 84% within the first month — driven primarily by the elimination of unplanned stoppages and the introduction of weight-based automatic rejection
  • The customer had production data for the first time — real output counts, shift reports, and reject rates — which allowed their management to make informed decisions on staffing, maintenance scheduling, and product quality improvement
  • The entire upgrade was completed without a single day of unplanned production loss

Period 3 — Emerson Electric (2023–2025)

After relocating to the United States, I joined Emerson Electric as a Senior Field Engineer — working on Emerson Ovation DCS and ControlLogix-based systems across power generation and water/wastewater treatment facilities. My work spanned the full engineering lifecycle: startup commissioning, live system upgrades, troubleshooting, and project delivery.

On DCS upgrade projects, I prepared formal technical submittals for customers and directly modified the control system across four layers:

  • Control logic — modifying sequences, interlocks, and PID loops in the Ovation controller
  • Graphics — building and updating operator displays and P&ID screens in the Ovation SCADA
  • Network architecture — reconfiguring EtherNet/IP, ControlNet, and Modbus communication
  • Database — restructuring tag databases, historian configuration, and alarm setpoints

All modifications were made on live operating systems — requiring careful change management and thorough pre-testing before any live action.

Troubleshooting Ovation DCS hardware, software, and industrial network faults on active facilities — diagnosing and resolving issues under time pressure on systems that could not be taken offline.

FAT and SAT leadership — led Factory Acceptance Tests and Site Acceptance Tests with customers, subcontractors, and inspectors. Prepared all test protocols, checklists, and presentations. Ran technical workshops and managed Q&A sessions through to resolution.

Key outcomes: led a major water treatment plant upgrade with zero downtime, and delivered multiple control systems with zero post-commissioning faults.

Area Detail
DCS installation & commissioning Installed, commissioned, and configured Ovation DCS hardware and software for startup projects. Set up I/O modules, field device connections, control strategies, and historian databases from ground up.
Ovation DCS upgrade projects Modified and prepared technical submittals for Ovation DCS upgrades — developed and enhanced monitoring displays, graphical P&ID tools, SCADA interfaces, and database configuration. Worked on live operating systems requiring zero disruption to production.
Industrial networks & ControlLogix Configured and troubleshot industrial communication networks including EtherNet/IP, ControlNet, and Modbus. Worked on ControlLogix-based hybrid control applications alongside Ovation DCS in mixed-platform environments.
Troubleshooting & diagnostics Troubleshot Ovation DCS hardware faults, network communication issues, and ControlLogix system problems on operating facilities. Resolved issues under time pressure on systems that could not be taken offline.
FAT, SAT & technical workshops Led Factory Acceptance Tests, Site Acceptance Tests, and technical workshops with cross-functional stakeholders — customers, subcontractors, inspectors, and engineering teams. Prepared all documentation, checklists, and presentations. Managed Q&A sessions and technical resolution of open items.
Project coordination Coordinated with contractors and engineering teams across multiple concurrent projects to ensure successful execution and on-time delivery. Acted as the technical interface between field teams and project management.
Water plant upgrade — zero downtime ★ Led a major water treatment plant control system upgrade without any interruption to plant operation. The facility served the public 24/7 — any downtime was unacceptable. Required meticulous phased planning, live system cutover strategy, and real-time coordination with plant operations staff throughout execution.
Zero post-commissioning faults ★ Delivered multiple high-performance control systems with zero post-commissioning faults — meaning no defects, no callbacks, no remediation visits required after handover. This is the standard I hold every project to.
Situation
  • City of San Diego wastewater treatment facility — running Emerson Ovation DCS
  • Required: integrate a new valve cluster into existing DCS — previously manual / standalone PLC
  • Challenge: integrating into a live, operating facility serving the public 24 hours a day
  • Zero process interruption was the non-negotiable requirement
Action
  • Owned full technical scope: DCS logic design, database config, HMI graphics, technical submittal, site commissioning
  • Designed 3 control modes: DCS AUTO / PLC LOCAL / MANUAL — each with I/O, logic, steps, and failover
  • All failure scenarios handled: alarms with procedures, safe fallback positions, hardware fail-safe
  • Submitted technical package to City of San Diego for approval before site work
Result
  • The valve cluster was successfully integrated into the Ovation DCS with all three control modes operational and fully tested during FAT and SAT
  • Zero disruption to plant operations during commissioning
  • The City of San Diego accepted the system with zero punch list items remaining at SAT sign-off
  • The failover procedures were documented and handed to the plant operations team as part of the formal documentation package
Technical Submittal
Sludge Pump Runtime-Based Auto-Selection System
Wastewater Treatment Facility — Ovation DCS Integration
Submittal No.: EMR-2024-WW-003
Rev: A
Prepared by: Ali Hajar Tehrani
Emerson Electric — Field Engineering
Client
City of San Diego
Facility
Wastewater Treatment Plant
Control system
Emerson Ovation DCS
System
3 × Sludge Transfer Pumps
1. Scope of Work

This submittal covers the design, configuration, and commissioning of an automatic pump selection and runtime equalization system for three (3) sludge transfer pumps (P-101, P-102, P-103) serving a common sludge holding tank. The system is integrated into the existing Emerson Ovation DCS and provides three operating modes: DCS Automatic, PLC Local, and Manual.

The primary control objective is to equalize runtime hours across all three pumps — automatically selecting the pump with the lowest accumulated runtime hours for the next duty cycle. This extends pump service life, distributes wear evenly, and reduces unplanned maintenance frequency.

2. System Description
Equipment
3 × Sludge transfer pumps: P-101, P-102, P-103
Common sludge holding tank (ST-01)
Individual motor starters with status feedback
Local control panel with PLC and HMI
Runtime hour meters (software-based in DCS + PLC)
Operating modes
DCS AUTO: Ovation DCS reads runtime counters from all 3 pumps, selects lowest-hours pump, starts automatically on tank level trigger
PLC LOCAL: Local PLC performs same runtime-based selection independently; control room monitors only
MANUAL: Operator selects pump via selector switch and starts via local RUN pushbutton
3. Runtime-Based Pump Selection Logic
Selection algorithm: On each pump start demand (triggered by tank level or manual call), the system compares the accumulated runtime hours of all available pumps (P-101, P-102, P-103). The pump with the lowest runtime hours among those not in fault or maintenance is selected as the duty pump. In the event of a tie (equal hours ± 0.1 hr), the pump with the lowest tag number is selected (P-101 priority over P-102 over P-103).
Parameter Tag / Register Data type Description
P101_RUN_HRSDCS tag / PLC D100REAL (hours, 0.1 resolution)Accumulated runtime hours P-101. Increments when motor status = RUN. Stored in non-volatile memory.
P102_RUN_HRSDCS tag / PLC D102REAL (hours, 0.1 resolution)Accumulated runtime hours P-102.
P103_RUN_HRSDCS tag / PLC D104REAL (hours, 0.1 resolution)Accumulated runtime hours P-103.
DUTY_PUMP_SELDCS tag / PLC D200INT (1, 2, or 3)Result of selection algorithm. Written before pump start command. 1=P-101, 2=P-102, 3=P-103.
LT-ST01-HHDCS AI tagBOOL (from AI level)Tank high-level trigger: level > 80% → initiate pump selection and start. Primary auto-start trigger.
LT-ST01-LLDCS AI tagBOOL (from AI level)Tank low-level stop: level < 20% → stop running pump. Runtime counter pauses on motor stop.
4. I/O Summary
Tag Description Type Signal Notes
LT-ST01Sludge tank level transmitterAI4–20mA, 0–100%Primary trigger for auto pump start/stop. HART-enabled.
P101-START / P102-START / P103-STARTPump start commands (×3)DO24VDC to MCCRelay output to motor control center (MCC) start coil.
P101-STOP / P102-STOP / P103-STOPPump stop commands (×3)DO24VDC to MCCRelay output to MCC stop coil.
P101-RUN / P102-RUN / P103-RUNPump running status feedback (×3)DI24VDC from MCC aux contactUsed to increment runtime counter. RUNNING = counter active.
P101-FAULT / P102-FAULT / P103-FAULTPump fault / trip status (×3)DI24VDC NC from MCC trip relayFault = pump excluded from selection. Alarm raised automatically.
HS-P101-MAN / HS-P102-MAN / HS-P103-MANManual pump selector switches (×3)DI24VDC selector switchUsed in MANUAL mode only. Selects which pump to start.
HS-RUN / HS-STOPManual RUN / STOP pushbuttonsDI24VDC momentaryPanel-mounted. Active in MANUAL and PLC modes.
MODE-SELMode selector switch (DCS / PLC / MAN)DI ×224VDC 3-position key switchTwo DI bits encode 3 positions: 00=DCS, 01=PLC, 10=MAN.
P101-MAINT / P102-MAINT / P103-MAINTMaintenance inhibit switches (×3)DI24VDC toggle switch on panelWhen ON: excludes that pump from auto-selection. For maintenance without full lockout.
YSS-ESTOPArea E-stop — sludge pump skidDI24VDC NC hardwired safety loopHardwired to all 3 MCC stop coils AND to DCS/PLC input. Overrides all modes.
5. Design Standards & Compliance
• ISA-18.2 — Alarm management
• ISA-5.1 — Instrumentation symbols
• NFPA 70 (NEC) — Electrical wiring
• City of San Diego SCADA standards
• Emerson Ovation DCS design guidelines
• EPA 40 CFR Part 503 — wastewater data logging
Situation
  • The wastewater treatment facility had three sludge transfer pumps serving a common sludge holding tank
  • Previously, operators manually selected which pump to run — leading to uneven wear, premature failure on the overused pumps, and higher maintenance costs
  • The customer required an automated system that would equalize runtime hours across all three pumps — always selecting the pump that had run the least — and integrate this logic into the existing Ovation DCS while also providing local PLC and manual backup modes
Action
  • Designed the full runtime equalization algorithm: compare P101/P102/P103 hours → select minimum → DUTY_PUMP_SEL
  • Built 3-mode architecture: DCS AUTO (cloud counters) / PLC LOCAL (Modbus sync) / MANUAL (selector switch)
  • Configured Ovation DCS database, HMI graphics, historian tags, alarm management
  • Wrote and submitted Technical Submittal EMR-2024-WW-003 to City of San Diego
  • Commissioned on live facility — zero downtime to sludge pumping operation
Result
  • Technical submittal approved by City of San Diego on first review with no comments
  • System commissioned on schedule with zero downtime
  • After six months of operation the three pumps showed runtime hours within 8% of each other — compared to a previous spread of over 300 hours between the most-used and least-used pump
  • Maintenance interval costs reduced proportionally
  • Zero post-commissioning faults
Situation
  • A new control system upgrade was being delivered to a live wastewater treatment facility
  • The DCS upgrade included new control logic modules that needed to be integrated into the existing Ovation DCS — which was actively running the plant
  • Simply adding untested logic to a live system was not acceptable
  • Any bug in the new logic could cause a real process fault, potentially shutting down critical infrastructure
  • The challenge was to test everything thoroughly before it touched the live system — and to integrate it module by module rather than all at once
Action
  • Structured commissioning in 2 phases: FAT at workshop → SAT at facility
  • Key discipline: modular logic design — each function built as a standalone module, tested in isolation first
  • Every module deployed in OUT_OF_SERVICE mode — runs logic internally, sends no output commands to field
  • Shadow mode: module watches the live process, calculates outputs, we compare before enabling
  • Modules enabled one at a time — 30-min observation window between each
  • At no point did untested code run on the live system
Result
  • Every module passed FAT before leaving the workshop
  • At SAT, the modular approach meant that each new function was verified at site before being enabled in the live DCS — the plant continued operating normally throughout the entire commissioning process
  • Final DCS integration was completed with zero process interruptions and zero post-commissioning faults
  • The customer signed the SAT acceptance certificate on the final day of the planned schedule with no open punch list items

Period 4 — Amazon Robotics (2025–Present)

Following my time at Emerson, I joined Amazon Robotics as a Product Support Engineer — a global role supporting the full Amazon Robotics PLC, controls, hardware, and complex systems portfolio in a 24/7 follow-the-sun on-call model. I serve as a Subject Matter Expert (SME) in robotic systems, transportation, and sortation technology, responding to escalations from Fulfillment Centers, Transportation Centers, and on-site engineers worldwide.

  • System support & troubleshooting — responding to low- and high-severity issues escalated from sites globally, diagnosing PLC, controls, and hardware faults across the Amazon Robotics portfolio
  • Incident management — leading response to large-scale issues impacting Amazon Robotics operations, driving root cause analysis and coordinating resolution from first alert to close
  • System maintenance & optimization — executing software upgrades, configuration changes, and optimizations across the robot fleet
  • Cross-functional collaboration — working with Amazon Robotics engineering, product, and operations teams to deliver short-term mitigations and long-term solutions
  • Tooling & dashboards — developing tools for ticket tracking, metrics, operational dashboards, and log analysis
  • Technical documentation — creating and maintaining troubleshooting guides, automation documentation, and diagnostic tools
  • Continuous improvement — analyzing recurring incidents to identify patterns and root causes, converting findings into improved documentation and tooling
  • Training — developing training materials and delivering sessions for other engineers covering Amazon Robotics systems, PLC and controls architecture, and troubleshooting methodology
Situation
  • Amazon Robotics Product Support had a general training program — one package covering all hardware and controls topics, designed for everyone: operators, technicians, and product support engineers alike
  • The problem was that these audiences are not at the same level and do not need the same information
  • A general course was either too advanced for operators who just needed to recognize and describe a fault, or not deep enough for PSEs who needed to diagnose and resolve complex control system issues remotely
  • On top of that, the existing content was recorded meeting sessions — screen recordings of training calls, not purpose-built explanatory material
  • And there was no way to know whether the training was working — completion rate was tracked, but whether an engineer could actually apply what they learned was not measured at all
Action
  • I redesigned the training from the ground up using four principles: pre-assess before training, target each audience separately, build real field-quality video content, and measure effectiveness with AI after training
1 — PRE-ASSESSMENT BEFORE TRAINING STARTS
Before any engineer or operator received a training module, I assessed their current knowledge level. If they were below the minimum baseline needed to understand the content — for example, a new engineer who had never worked with PLC systems before — I assigned them a prerequisite module first to bring them up to the required level. Training only started once they had the foundation to actually absorb it. Without this step, engineers would watch the videos and not understand enough to apply anything.
Assessment result Action Goal
At or above baseline Assigned directly to the relevant training module for their role. No wasted time on content they already know.
Below baseline Assigned prerequisite module(s) first. Re-assessed before main training begins. Engineer has the foundation to actually understand and apply the main training.
2 — AUDIENCE-TARGETED MODULES
The same technology is covered for all audiences — but at different depth, with different focus, and with different expected outcomes. Each audience receives only the content relevant to their role. The modules are independent — updating one does not affect others.
Audience Topics & depth Expected outcome after training
Operators What each component does at a functional level. What normal vs. abnormal looks like. How to read status indicators. When to call support. What NOT to touch. Recognize a fault, describe it accurately to the support team, and avoid making it worse.
Technicians Hardware components, replacement procedures, safety steps, basic diagnostics, maintenance routines. Execute standard maintenance and first-level hardware fixes independently without calling a PSE.
Product Support Engineers PLC logic and control architecture, fault code interpretation, log analysis, network topology, known failure modes with root causes, documented troubleshooting methodology. Diagnose and resolve issues remotely using the documented approach — without escalating. Faster resolution, fewer callbacks.
3 — REAL FIELD VIDEO CONTENT
The previous training was recorded meeting sessions — someone sharing their screen on a call, talking through slides or a computer demo. It was not made for independent learning. I replaced this entirely with purpose-built video content recorded in the field and in the lab — real hardware, real systems, real issues.
Old format New format
Recorded Zoom/Teams training session — presenter sharing slides or screen. Background noise, interruptions, Q&A breaks mid-content. Made for a live audience, not self-paced learners. Filmed on-site in the field and in the lab. Camera on the actual hardware, device, or system being explained. I walk through each component, demonstrate each troubleshooting step, and explain directly on camera — on the real system, not a simulation.
Generic — same recording used for all audiences regardless of role or knowledge level. Audience-specific — PSE video shows fault codes and control logic in detail. Operator video shows the same device but explains what to look for and who to call. Different narration, different depth, same real hardware.
Watched once at training time, then no reference value — hard to find a specific segment when needed on the job. Modular — each video covers exactly one topic. Engineers can go back to a specific module during a live issue and watch just the 5-minute segment on the exact fault they are dealing with.
4 — AI QUALITY CHECK & UPDATE LOOP
Completing the video is not the goal — being able to solve the issue using the documented approach is the goal. After an engineer completes a module, AI monitors their real support tickets to check if they are actually applying what the training taught.
Step What happens Result
After module completion AI monitors the engineer's support tickets for the next 30 days. Checks: are they using the troubleshooting approach from the training? Resolving faster? Escalating less? Effectiveness score per engineer per module.
Engineer passes AI confirms they are using the documented methodology and resolving issues faster. Module marked effectively completed. Engineer certified. No further action.
Engineer does not pass AI flags that despite completing the module, the engineer is not applying the approach or not resolving faster. Could be the engineer, or could be the training content itself. Engineer flagged for targeted follow-up. Module flagged for review.
Module fails across multiple engineers The training content is the problem. I go back to the field, re-record the module with better explanation or different examples, and re-assign. The cycle restarts. Self-improving curriculum driven by real field performance data, not trainer intuition.
Result
  • Training is now relevant to the person receiving it, at the right level, in a format they can use on the job
  • Engineers who were below baseline got the foundation they needed before the main content — improving comprehension and retention
  • The field-recorded video format means engineers can watch a 5-minute segment on the exact hardware or fault they are dealing with during a live support case — the training becomes a reference tool, not just a one-time watch
  • The AI quality check replaced completion rate with field performance as the primary metric, and the update loop means the curriculum improves continuously based on whether engineers are actually solving issues faster
Situation
  • As a Product Support Engineer at Amazon Robotics, responding to high-severity incidents on a 24/7 global system is only half the job
  • The other half is making sure the same issue never comes back
  • The challenge was that incident response and root cause analysis were often treated as separate activities — engineers would resolve an issue and close the ticket, but without a structured RCA process the underlying cause frequently resurfaced in a different site or a different system
  • The work was reactive instead of systematic
Action — 6-phase RCA
  • Phase 1 — Problem definition: what failed, where, when, how often, measured impact
  • Phase 2 — Data collection: logs, timeline, change log, interviews — evidence before hypotheses
  • Phase 3 — Cause identification: Fishbone (6 branches) + 5 Whys + FMEA scoring
  • Phase 4 — Root cause determination: test each candidate against all evidence, build causal chain
  • Phase 5 — Corrective action: containment first, then permanent fix with owner + deadline
  • Phase 6 — Verification: 30-day monitoring, lessons-learned published, knowledge base updated
RCA PROCESS — 6 PHASES
# Phase Objective Key activities Tools used Quality gate — must be met before next phase
01 Problem Definition Define the problem precisely — what failed, where, when, how often, and what the impact was. Separate symptom from problem. · Write a single-sentence problem statement (what, where, when, how bad)
· Quantify impact: downtime minutes, units affected, sites impacted
· Confirm the problem is real and repeatable — not a one-off
· Define what "solved" looks like before starting analysis 		Problem statement template
IS / IS NOT analysis
Impact log		 Clear written problem statement agreed by all stakeholders. Impact quantified. Scope boundary defined.
02 Data Collection Gather all available evidence before forming any hypothesis. Evidence must be captured before it disappears. · Pull system logs, historian data, alarm logs from time of incident
· Build a timeline: what happened, in what order, at what time
· Review maintenance records and recent changes (firmware, config, hardware swaps)
· Interview engineers and operators who were present
· Photograph or preserve any physical evidence 		Incident timeline diagram
Sequence-of-events log
Change log review
Historian trend export		 Timeline documented. All relevant data collected. No hypotheses formed yet — this phase is evidence only.
03 Cause Identification Identify all possible contributing causes — not just the most obvious one. Categorize by People, Process, Equipment, Environment. · Build Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram — branches: Hardware, Software, Process, Human, Environment, Design
· List all possible causes per branch without filtering
· Apply 5 Whys to each candidate cause — ask "why" until a systemic factor is reached
· Use FMEA to assess severity × occurrence × detectability for each cause
· Use Pareto analysis to rank causes by frequency/impact if data volume allows 		Fishbone diagram
5 Whys worksheet
FMEA table
Pareto chart		 All candidate causes documented. Each branch of Fishbone explored. No cause eliminated without data-based justification.
04 Root Cause Determination Narrow down from all possible causes to the verified root cause — the one that, if eliminated, prevents recurrence. · Test each candidate cause against the evidence — can it explain ALL aspects of the problem?
· Eliminate causes that don't fit all data points
· Verify root cause by confirming: removing it prevents recurrence (in test or simulation)
· Distinguish root cause from contributing causes and proximate (immediate) causes
· Confirm causal chain: root cause → contributing factors → immediate cause → problem 		Causal chain diagram
Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)
Evidence matrix
Hypothesis testing		 Root cause verified against all collected data. Causal chain documented. Stakeholders agree on root cause before moving to solutions.
05 Corrective Action Design and implement solutions that eliminate the root cause — not just suppress the symptom. · Generate multiple solution options — engineering fix, process change, software update, training, design change
· Evaluate each: effectiveness, feasibility, cost, time, side effects
· Assign owner, timeline, and success metric to every action item
· Implement short-term containment action first (stops bleeding) then long-term fix (removes root cause)
· Update documentation, SOPs, and troubleshooting guides with new knowledge 		Action item tracker
RACI matrix
Containment plan
Change control form		 Every action has an owner, deadline, and measurable success criterion. Containment active. Permanent fix scheduled.
06 Verification & Closure Confirm the fix worked and the problem has not recurred. Share findings to prevent the same issue at other sites. · Monitor for recurrence over a defined period (e.g., 30 days post-fix)
· Verify measurable improvement vs. baseline (downtime, frequency, resolution time)
· Conduct follow-up audit or test to confirm fix is stable
· Publish lessons-learned document and share across all affected sites
· Add to knowledge base — convert RCA output into searchable ticket reference for future similar issues 		30-day recurrence monitor
Metric comparison
Lessons-learned report
Knowledge base entry		 No recurrence in monitoring period. Metrics confirm improvement. Lessons-learned published and shared. Ticket closed with RCA reference.
RCA TOOLS — WHEN TO USE WHICH
Tool Best used when How it works In Amazon Robotics context
5 Whys Single, clear failure with a traceable cause chain. Quick analysis needed. Ask "why did this happen?" 5 times in sequence. Each answer becomes the next question until a systemic cause is reached. E.g. Robot stops → why? Drive fault → why? Motor overcurrent → why? Obstructed path → why? Sensor missed obstruction → why? Sensor calibration drift. Root cause: calibration interval too long.
Fishbone (Ishikawa) Multiple possible causes across different categories. Complex or recurring issue. Draw a fish skeleton. Problem at head. Bones = categories (Hardware, Software, Process, Human, Environment, Design). Team brainstorms causes per category. Used when a conveyor fault has multiple contributing factors — mechanical wear, firmware config, operator error, and environmental vibration all identified as branches before drilling down.
FMEA (Failure Mode & Effects Analysis) Proactive analysis before a failure occurs, or prioritizing multiple identified failure modes. For each failure mode: score Severity (1–10) × Occurrence (1–10) × Detectability (1–10) = Risk Priority Number (RPN). Address highest RPN first. Used to prioritize which recurring issue class to address first when multiple failure modes are identified across the fleet — highest RPN gets engineering escalation, lower ones get documentation updates.
Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) Complex system with multiple failure paths. Safety-critical issues. Understanding how multiple causes combine. Start from the undesired top event. Work down using AND/OR logic gates to identify all combinations of events that could produce it. Identifies single points of failure. Applied to high-severity incidents where a system outage could have resulted from multiple simultaneous faults — maps the AND conditions (e.g., both primary and backup path failed) that allowed the event to occur.
Pareto Analysis Large volume of incidents. Prioritizing which causes or categories to fix first. Plot cause categories by frequency or impact. The 80/20 rule typically applies — 80% of incidents come from 20% of causes. Fix the top-frequency causes first. Used quarterly to analyze ticket data across all sites — which fault codes account for the majority of resolution time? Those become the priority for new training modules and engineering fixes.
COMMON RCA MISTAKES — WHAT TO AVOID
Mistake Why it happens Correct approach
Fixing the symptom, not the cause Time pressure. "Just make it work again." Closing the ticket feels like solving the problem. Always separate containment (stop the bleeding) from corrective action (remove the cause). Both are required. One without the other is incomplete.
Jumping to solutions before cause is confirmed The first cause that seems plausible gets acted on. Saves time — but if wrong, problem recurs. Complete phases 1–4 before moving to phase 5. A solution implemented against the wrong root cause wastes time and may mask the real issue.
Stopping 5 Whys too early The first "why" answer sounds technical enough to be a root cause — but it's still a symptom. Keep asking why until you reach a systemic or organizational cause — something that can be changed permanently. If the answer is still a hardware or software state, keep going.
Blaming people instead of systems Human error is often the proximate cause — it gets named and the analysis stops there. Human error is almost always the result of a system that allowed or caused the error. Ask: why was the error possible? What process, design, or training gap enabled it?
No verification or closure Fix is implemented and team moves on. No monitoring period. Recurrence goes unnoticed or is treated as a new incident. Phase 6 is mandatory. Define the monitoring period and metric before implementing the fix. A fix is not confirmed until the problem has not recurred in the monitored window.
Challenges
  • The hardest part of applying structured RCA in a fast-paced 24/7 support environment is time pressure
  • The instinct during a live incident is to fix the symptom and close the ticket — the system is down, customers are impacted, and stopping to document a causal chain feels like slowing down
  • The discipline is in separating the two activities: containment happens immediately and in parallel, but RCA runs as a structured follow-up
  • A second challenge is data availability — logs expire, system states get overwritten, and the longer the gap between incident and investigation, the less evidence there is
  • Building the habit of capturing evidence during or immediately after the incident is critical to making RCA possible at all
Result
  • Applying this structured RCA methodology converted recurring incidents from repeated fire-fighting events into closed engineering problems
  • Each completed RCA produced a verified root cause, a permanent corrective action with an owner and deadline, and a lessons-learned entry in the knowledge base that prevented the same issue from consuming support time at other sites
  • Over time, the pattern-based analysis (Pareto across ticket data) also drove the training module roadmap — the highest-frequency unresolved issue classes became the next training modules to build
Situation
  • Amazon Fulfillment Centers (FCs) running Gen 11 and Gen 12 technology deploy between 200 and 300 pick-and-place robots per facility — typically ABB collaborative robots integrated into a high-speed sortation and fulfillment line
  • When one of these robots faults or goes offline, it doesn't just affect one workstation: depending on the FC layout, a single robot outage can cascade upstream and downstream, stalling throughput across an entire zone
  • As the SME on-call, I am responsible for live remote troubleshooting of these robots — diagnosing the fault, directing the on-site engineer, and restoring operation as fast as possible
  • The fault domain for these robots spans 8 categories, each requiring a different diagnostic approach
Action — structured triage
  • Step 1 — Field checklist: give on-site engineer specific yes/no checks, get results for every item
  • Step 2 — Log review in parallel: robot controller, PLC, network, IPC, alarms, safety
  • Step 3 — System layer check: Sortation Planning → Perception → Motion Planning → Safety → Control → Hardware → Network → Upstream/Downstream
  • Identify faulted layer → escalate with specific evidence, not a vague description
  • 8 fault domains — each with its own diagnostic checklist and escalation path
Context: I am remote. The on-site person is a technician or field engineer. I direct them precisely — they are my eyes and hands in the field. Every step below produces a specific observation that either confirms or eliminates a hypothesis. Speed matters: the FC is live.
TROUBLESHOOTING PROCESS — 3 STAGES
# Stage What I do What I am looking for
01 Field checklist
engineer on-site runs it		 I give the on-site engineer or technician a targeted checklist immediately — based on the alarm description or fault code. Each item is a specific, unambiguous observation: LED color, indicator state, a yes/no check, a reading from a gauge or display. I do not ask open-ended questions like "what does it look like?" I ask: "Is the green RUN LED on or off?" They go through every item and report back results for all of them — not just the ones that look wrong.

Example field checklist items (robot station):
□ Robot controller power LED — green / red / off?
□ FlexPendant — is there an active alarm message? Read the exact code.
□ E-stop mushroom buttons in this zone — any pressed/stuck?
□ Safety scanner indicator LED — green / amber / red?
□ Network cable at robot controller — seated and locked?
□ Induction conveyor upstream — is it running and delivering items?
□ Downstream sortation conveyor — running and clear?
□ Any visible mechanical obstruction in robot workspace?
□ Gripper or end-of-arm tool — any visible damage or loose connection?
□ Vision camera — any indicator LED? Any visible obstruction of the lens?
Physical causes that are invisible in any log — a cable pulled loose, a stuck E-stop, a fogged camera lens, a jam on the upstream conveyor. This step frequently finds the answer immediately and avoids 20 minutes of log analysis.
02 My log review
I pull & analyze in parallel		 While the field checklist is being run, I pull and review all available logs simultaneously. I am building a timeline: what happened first, what followed, what is the causal sequence? The sources I check:

Log source What I look for Key question
Robot controller logABB error codes, axis faults, safety events, RAPID task exceptions, timestampsWhat was the first fault? Were there prior warnings?
PLC diagnostic bufferFault codes, I/O state at time of fault, communication errors, motion faultsDoes PLC timestamp correlate with robot fault?
Network logSwitch port errors, packet loss, heartbeat drops, DHCP eventsDid network fault precede or follow the robot fault?
IPC / application logJob dispatch log, state machine transitions, application exceptions or crashesWas a job dispatched? Did the state machine get stuck?
Alarm & event logAll active and historical alarms for this robot and surrounding zoneAre other robots in this zone also faulting? (Fleet signal)
Safety controller logZone events, E-stop history, speed monitoring violations, safety CRC statusAny safety event is always investigated — never assumed spurious
The sequence and timing of events across all sources. A network drop 2 seconds before the robot fault is a very different situation from a robot fault that caused a network connection loss. Sequence determines the causal direction.
03 System communication check
layer by layer		 Using the log data and field results, I check the communication and status of each system layer in sequence. For each layer I have a specific checklist. The layers — and their checklists — are shown in the reference table below.

Key principle: I do not guess which layer is the problem. I work through them in order — starting with the layers most likely to cause the observed symptom — and confirm healthy or faulted for each one before calling a specialist team. When I escalate, I bring the specific layer, the specific check that failed, and the specific log evidence. I do not escalate with "the robot is broken."
Which system layer lost communication or produced incorrect output — and whether that layer is the root cause or was itself caused by a layer above or below it.
SYSTEM LAYER CHECKLISTS — 8 LAYERS
Each layer has a specific checklist. I run through each one in order, marking ✅ HEALTHY or ❌ FAILED. The first layer that fails becomes the investigation focus. Once the failed layer is identified, I escalate with the checklist result — not a vague description.
TICKET CORRESPONDENCE SOP — STANDARDIZED SUMMARY FORMAT
Purpose: Every ticket update follows the same structure so any manager, engineer, or on-call successor can read the summary and immediately understand: what is happening, what was checked, what was found, what action was taken, and what the next step is. No one should have to read through a thread of messages to understand the current state.
Field What to write Example
🔴 SYMPTOM One sentence: what the robot or system is doing (or not doing). Observable behavior only — no guessing here. Robot #47, Station C3 — not picking. In READY state, receiving items, not initiating pick cycle. No active alarms on FlexPendant.
📋 FIELD CHECK List each field checklist item and its result. Format: item → PASS / FAIL / VALUE. Do not skip any items. Controller power LED → GREEN ✅ | E-stop buttons → ALL CLEAR ✅ | Safety scanner LED → GREEN ✅ | Network cable → SEATED ✅ | Upstream conveyor → RUNNING ✅ | Downstream conveyor → RUNNING ✅ | Vision camera LED → OFF ❌
📊 LOGS REVIEWED List each log source checked and key finding. If nothing found, write "No errors." Do not omit sources. Robot controller log → No error codes ✅ | PLC buffer → No faults ✅ | Network log → No packet loss ✅ | IPC app log → Job dispatch confirmed, no pick response received ❌ | Alarm log → No fleet-wide events ✅ | Safety log → No events ✅
🔍 LAYER STATUS For each system layer: HEALTHY / FAILED / NOT CHECKED. State which layer is the focus. Sortation Planning ✅ | Perception ❌ FAILED (vision camera indicator off, no pose returned in perception log) | Motion Planning — not checked (blocked by perception) | Safety ✅ | Control System ✅ | Hardware ✅ | Network ✅ | Upstream/Downstream ✅
💡 HYPOTHESIS Current best explanation based on evidence. One sentence. Label it as hypothesis, not fact, until confirmed. Hypothesis: Vision camera power or status fault. Camera indicator LED is off — camera may not be powered or has lost connection to perception system. Perception layer confirmed failed, all other layers healthy.
⚡ ACTION TAKEN What was done, by whom, at what time. Specific actions only — no vague "investigated the issue." 14:32 — Directed on-site tech to check camera power cable at robot controller cabinet. Cable found loose at port. Re-seated cable. 14:35 — Camera LED returned GREEN. Perception system confirmed returning poses. Robot resumed picking.
✅ RESOLUTION / STATUS Current state: RESOLVED / IN PROGRESS / ESCALATED / MONITORING. If resolved: root cause confirmed. If escalated: who was called and what was handed off. RESOLVED 14:37. Root cause: loose camera power cable at controller cabinet. Robot #47 returned to normal operation. Monitoring for 30 min to confirm stability.
🔁 NEXT STEP / FOLLOW-UP What happens next. If resolved: RCA needed? Documentation update? If in progress: what is the next action and ETA? Follow-up: Check cable retention on all robots in this zone (same hardware batch). If pattern found across multiple robots → open RCA ticket for cable retention design issue. Update troubleshooting guide: add "vision camera LED" to field checklist step 1.
EXAMPLE TICKET SUMMARY (as written in the ticketing system)
TICKET #AR-2024-4471 — SUMMARY UPDATE — 14:37 PST — Ali Hajar Tehrani
🔴 SYMPTOM: Robot #47 / Station C3 / FC-SEA-7 / Gen 12 — not picking. READY state, items arriving, pick cycle not initiating. No FlexPendant alarms.
📋 FIELD CHECK: All clear EXCEPT vision camera indicator LED = OFF (all other items passed).
📊 LOGS: IPC app log — job dispatched, no pick confirmation received. Perception log — no pose output since 13:58. All other logs clean.
🔍 FAILED LAYER: Perception — camera LED off, no pose output. All other 7 layers healthy.
⚡ ACTION: 14:32 directed on-site tech to inspect camera power cable at controller cabinet. Cable found loose. Re-seated 14:35. Camera LED GREEN. Perception resumed.
✅ RESOLUTION: RESOLVED 14:37. Root cause: loose camera power cable. Robot back in service. Monitoring 30 min.
🔁 FOLLOW-UP: Inspect camera cables on all Gen 12 robots in SEA-7 Zone C. If pattern found → open RCA. Update field checklist to include camera LED check.
System context: ABB pick-and-place robots, 200–300 units per FC (Gen 11 / Gen 12). Each robot connects to a PLC (motion control and I/O), a vision system (item identification and pick pose), a safety controller (area monitoring and speed supervision), and the FC network (job dispatch and telemetry). Upstream: induction conveyor delivers items to the robot's pick zone. Downstream: sortation conveyor or chute receives placed items. A fault in any layer — or in the upstream/downstream handoff — can take a robot offline.
Challenges
  • Three things make live troubleshooting of a 200–300 robot fleet especially demanding
  • First, speed — every minute a robot is offline in a fulfillment center has a measurable impact on throughput
  • Diagnosis has to be fast and accurate, which means knowing the fault domains well enough to triage in seconds from a log or alarm description, not minutes
  • Second, remote diagnosis — I am not physically at the FC
  • I have to direct an on-site technician who may have varying levels of experience, which means giving precise, step-by-step instructions that produce clear pass/fail observations
  • Third, fleet correlation — a single robot fault is sometimes a symptom of a fleet-wide issue (shared network segment, common firmware, same hardware batch)
  • Recognizing when an individual fault is actually a systemic signal and escalating early is critical to preventing a wider outage
Result
  • Structured domain-based triage reduced time-to-diagnosis on live calls
  • By knowing which domain to rule in or out first based on the fault code and alarm description, the diagnostic path is shortened significantly compared to unstructured investigation
  • Recurring fault patterns identified during live calls also fed directly into the RCA process and training module roadmap — converting one-off resolutions into documented institutional knowledge available to the entire global support team

Why Siemens Energy

How to read this: The full Siemens Energy job description is shown below exactly as written. Green highlight = directly matched by my experience. Yellow highlight = partially matched / transferable skill. Unformatted text = areas I will grow into in this role.
Role overview

As a Sr. Manufacturing Engineer with Siemens Energy's Blades and Vanes team, you implement, drive, and own Automation & Digitalization initiatives within our investment casting facility. You work hands-on to modernize manufacturing processes, improving efficiency, quality, and data visibility across operations. A key part of your role is leading manufacturing automation, including advanced robotics, automated shell lines, and production automation. You partner closely with operations, engineering, and digital teams to take ideas from concept through implementation. Each day, you help turn smart manufacturing solutions into real, measurable improvements on the shop floor.

How you'll make an impact
Automation & Manufacturing Systems
  • Design and optimize automation solutions for investment casting processes (wax, shell, melt, pour, finishing, inspection). ↳ 17 yrs designing automation from scratch across food, packaging, water treatment
  • Enhance PLC, HMI, SCADA systems for improved reliability and automation throughout. ↳ S7-300, WinCC, Ovation DCS — hands-on across all three layers
  • Identify opportunities to reduce manual effort and waste through automation. ↳ Core of the Tamin Online legacy factory retrofit story — 5 manual stations → fully automated line
  • Collaborate with maintenance and engineering on controls standards and system upgrades. ↳ Tetra Pak, Tamin Online, Emerson — coordinating with engineering and maintenance teams across every project for 17 years
Digitalization & Smart Manufacturing
  • Lead digital transformation initiatives, data collection, digital work instructions, and process dashboards. ↳ Amazon Robotics: built dashboards, ticket tracking tools, and log analysis systems
  • Develop data pipelines from shop-floor systems to analytics platforms. ↳ WinCC Historian → OPC-UA pipelines; Python + SQL skills for analytics layer
  • Support Industry 4.0 concepts like connected equipment and predictive insights. ↳ OPC-UA, networked PLC systems, SCADA historian — foundational I4.0 building blocks
Process Improvement & Collaboration
  • Partner with operations, quality, IT, and engineering to translate business needs into technical solutions. ↳ Every project — customer requirements → technical design → implementation
  • Support continuous improvement initiatives (Lean, Six Sigma). ↳ Amazon Robotics: systematic CI process on recurring incidents; Tamin Online: RCA-driven PM reducing downtime 30–50%
  • Provide technical leadership for pilot projects and full-scale deployments and train staff in new systems and digital tools. ↳ Led all FAT/SAT + operator training at Tetra Pak, Tamin, Emerson; Amazon training programs
What you bring — requirements
  • Bachelor's degree in engineering (Automation, Electrical, Industrial, Manufacturing, Mechatronics, or similar). ↳ BSc Electrical Engineering + MBA
  • 5 or more years of experience in manufacturing automation and/or digitalization. ↳ 17 years — exceeds requirement by 3×
  • Hands-on experience with PLCs (Siemens, Allen-Bradley, or equivalent), HMI/SCADA systems, and industrial networks. ↳ Siemens S7-300 / TIA Portal, WinCC, Ovation DCS, EtherNet/IP, Profibus, OPC-UA
  • Strong understanding of manufacturing environments (foundry or heavy industrial preferred). ↳ Food processing, water treatment, robotics — all high-stakes process environments
  • Basic knowledge of Lean Manufacturing, Six Sigma, or Opex frameworks. ↳ Applied CI and waste-reduction principles throughout career — formal Lean/Six Sigma certification to follow
  • Applicants must be legally authorized for employment in the United States. ✅ Green Card holder — no sponsorship required
Preferred qualifications
  • Proficiency in Python, SQL, Power BI, or similar analytics tools. ↳ Python + SQL actively used at Amazon Robotics for tooling and dashboards
  • Project management experience in automation or digital initiatives. ↳ 17 years leading end-to-end automation projects — from scoping to SAT sign-off
14
Requirements fully matched
3
Transferable / partial match
17
Years of relevant experience
